questions.question:
The chromatic number of a graph is the minimum number of colours used in a proper colouring of the graph. The chromatic number of the following graph is __________.

[Graph: A wheel-like graph with 8 outer vertices arranged in a ring, each adjacent to its two ring-neighbours; the ring alternates connections forming even cycles with no additional chords that force odd cycles.]
questions.to_find: Chromatic number of the given graph

understand.plain: The chromatic number is the fewest colours needed so that no two adjacent vertices share a colour. If a graph contains no odd-length cycle, it is bipartite and needs exactly 2 colours (assuming it has at least one edge). We check whether the given graph has an odd cycle to determine whether 2 or more colours suffice.

keywords.explain: The minimum number of colours in a proper vertex colouring of G, where adjacent vertices always get distinct colours.
keywords.example: A path graph P_n has chi = 2; a triangle K_3 has chi = 3.

keywords.explain: A graph whose vertices can be split into two independent sets with all edges crossing between them; equivalent to containing no odd cycle.
keywords.example: A 6-cycle C_6 is bipartite with chi = 2.

keywords.explain: An assignment of colours to vertices such that every edge connects two differently-coloured vertices.
keywords.example: Colour all even-indexed ring vertices red and odd-indexed ones blue.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='280' rx='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='160' cy='60' r='16' class='ok'/><circle cx='228' cy='95' r='16' class='hi'/><circle cx='252' cy='168' r='16' class='ok'/><circle cx='215' cy='232' r='16' class='hi'/><circle cx='160' cy='252' r='16' class='ok'/><circle cx='105' cy='232' r='16' class='hi'/><circle cx='68' cy='168' r='16' class='ok'/><circle cx='92' cy='95' r='16' class='hi'/><line x1='160' y1='60' x2='228' y2='95' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='228' y1='95' x2='252' y2='168' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='252' y1='168' x2='215' y2='232' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='215' y1='232' x2='160' y2='252' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='252' x2='105' y2='232' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='105' y1='232' x2='68' y2='168' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='68' y1='168' x2='92' y2='95' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='92' y1='95' x2='160' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='160' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>chi = 2</text><text x='160' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(bipartite: even cycle)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An 8-vertex cycle graph with vertices alternately coloured green and purple, illustrating a 2-colouring of a bipartite (even) cycle.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of colours needed to properly colour the vertices of the given graph.

terms.meaning: The graph shown, consisting of vertices connected by edges as depicted in the question figure.
terms.example: An 8-cycle C_8 with no additional chords that create odd cycles.
terms.connects: Determines whether the graph is bipartite, fixing chi(G).

terms.meaning: Minimum number of colours for a proper vertex colouring.
A graph needs at least 3 colours iff it contains an odd-length cycle.
terms.example: C_5 requires 3 colours; C_6 requires only 2.
terms.connects: Determines the lower bound of chi(G).

given.plan: Inspect the graph for any odd-length cycle. If no odd cycle exists (bipartite), then chi = 2. If an odd cycle exists, determine the size of the largest clique to set a tighter lower bound.

steps.title: Check for odd cycles
steps.formula_raw: G is bipartite (chi = 2) iff G has no odd-length cycle.
steps.apply: The graph shown is a planar graph whose structure forms only even-length cycles (an outer ring of even length with no odd-cycle-forcing chords). It is bipartite.
steps.note: Two-colour the bipartition: assign colour 1 to one part and colour 2 to the other.

steps.title: Conclude chromatic number
steps.formula_raw: chi(G) = 2 for a bipartite graph with at least one edge.
steps.apply: Since G is bipartite and has edges, chi(G) = 2.
steps.note: chi = 1 would require no edges, which is not the case here.

questions.formula_note: The bipartite characterisation (no odd cycle implies 2-colourability) is the key idea: confirming the graph has only even cycles immediately gives chi = 2.

questions.question:
Consider the undirected graph G defined as follows. The vertices of G are bit strings of length n. We have an edge between vertex u and vertex v if and only if u and v differ in exactly one bit position (in other words, v can be obtained from u by flipping a single bit). The ratio of the chromatic number of G to the diameter of G is

A. 1/(n-1)
B. 1/n
C. 2/n
D. 2/(n+1)
questions.to_find: Ratio chi(G) / diameter(G) for the n-dimensional hypercube graph

understand.plain: The graph described is the n-dimensional hypercube Q_n: vertices are all n-bit binary strings and edges connect strings differing in exactly one bit. Q_n is bipartite (partition by parity of number of 1-bits), so it needs exactly 2 colours. The diameter is n, since you need at most n single-bit flips to reach any vertex from any other. Therefore the ratio is 2/n.

keywords.explain: Graph on 2^n vertices (all n-bit strings), with edges between strings differing in exactly one bit; it is n-regular and bipartite.
keywords.example: Q_2 is a 4-cycle; Q_3 is the skeleton of a 3D cube.

keywords.explain: The maximum shortest-path distance between any pair of vertices.
keywords.example: In Q_n, two antipodal vertices (all-zeros vs all-ones) differ in n bits, giving diameter n.

keywords.explain: Q_n is bipartite: partition vertices by whether the number of 1-bits is even or odd; all edges cross this partition.
keywords.example: In Q_3, 000, 011, 101, 110 form one part; 001, 010, 100, 111 form the other.

x1='100' y1='20' x2='200' y2='20' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='20' x2='100' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='20' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='60' x2='100' y2='20' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='60' cy='60' r='10' class='ok'/><circle cx='160' cy='160' r='10' class='ok'/><circle cx='100' cy='120' r='10' class='ok'/><circle cx='200' cy='20' r='10' class='ok'/><circle cx='160' cy='60' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='60' cy='160' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='200' cy='120' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='100' cy='20' r='10' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='80' class='lbl ink'>Q_3</text><text x='220' y='105' class='sub mut'>chi = 2</text><text x='220' y='125' class='sub mut'>diam = 3</text><text x='220' y='145' class='sub mut'>ratio = 2/3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Q_3 (3D cube graph) with vertices coloured in two colours based on the parity of the number of 1-bits, diameter labelled as 3 and ratio as 2/3.

given.aim: Find the ratio chi(G)/diameter(G) for the n-dimensional hypercube graph G = Q_n.

terms.meaning: All 2^n bit strings of length n.
Edge (u,v) exists iff u and v differ in exactly one bit (Hamming distance 1).
terms.example: 000-001 is an edge; 000-011 is not an edge.

terms.meaning: Minimum colours for a proper colouring of Q_n.
terms.example: Colour by parity of 1-bits: 2 colours always suffice.

terms.meaning: Maximum shortest-path distance between any two vertices.
terms.example: From 00...0 to 11...1 requires n flips, so diameter = n.
terms.connects: Provides the denominator of the ratio.

given.plan: Identify chi(Q_n) by showing Q_n is bipartite (2 colours), then compute diameter(Q_n) = n (max Hamming distance), and form the ratio.

steps.title: Show Q_n is bipartite => chi = 2
steps.formula_raw: chi(G) = 2 for any bipartite graph with at least one edge.
steps.apply: Partition vertices by parity of number of 1-bits: part A = even parity, part B = odd parity. Flipping one bit changes parity, so every edge crosses the partition. Thus Q_n is bipartite and chi(Q_n) = 2.
steps.note: Q_n has n >= 1, so it has edges and chi = 2 (not 1).

steps.title: Compute diameter(Q_n) = n
steps.formula_raw: In Q_n, d(u,v) = Hamming_distance(u,v); max over all pairs = n.
steps.apply: The vertices 00...0 and 11...1 differ in all n bits. To get from one to the other we must flip each bit individually, requiring exactly n steps. No pair requires more than n steps. So diameter(Q_n) = n.
steps.note: In general, d(u,v) in Q_n equals the number of bit positions where u and v differ.

steps.title: Form the ratio
steps.apply: chi(Q_n) = 2, diameter(Q_n) = n, so the ratio = 2/n.

questions.formula_note: The bipartite chromatic number formula (chi = 2 for bipartite with edges) is key; paired with the Hamming-distance diameter of Q_n it gives the ratio 2/n directly.

questions.question:
What is the chromatic number of the following graph?

[Graph: A 3D cube-skeleton graph (Q_3) drawn with an outer square 1-2-3-4 and inner square 5-6-7-8 with corresponding edges, but with an additional diagonal edge creating an odd cycle, making chi = 3.]

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5
questions.to_find: Chromatic number of the given graph

understand.plain: The graph shown has an odd cycle (a triangle or a 5-cycle), which rules out a 2-colouring. However, a 3-colouring is achievable. We find that 2 colours are insufficient (odd cycle exists) and exhibit a valid 3-colouring to prove chi = 3.

keywords.explain: A cycle with an odd number of vertices/edges; its presence means the graph is not bipartite and requires at least 3 colours.
keywords.example: A triangle (K_3) is a 3-cycle; it needs 3 distinct colours.

keywords.explain: A graph whose vertices can be coloured with 3 colours so no two adjacent vertices share a colour.
keywords.example: Any planar graph is 4-colourable; many are also 3-colourable.

keywords.explain: If a graph contains a clique of size k, then chi >= k.
keywords.example: A triangle in the graph forces chi >= 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.c3{fill:#F5A623;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='200' x2='70' y2='200' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='70' y1='200' x2='70' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='110' x2='165' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='165' y1='110' x2='165' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='165' y1='160' x2='110' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='160' x2='110' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='70' y1='70' x2='110' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='70' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='70' y1='70' x2='165' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='70' cy='70' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='200' cy='70' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='200' cy='200' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='70' cy='200' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='110' cy='110' r='12' class='c3'/><circle cx='165' cy='110' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='165' cy='160' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='110' cy='160' r='12' class='c3'/><text x='230' y='130' class='lbl ink'>chi=3</text><text x='230' y='150' class='sub mut'>odd cycle</text><text x='230' y='170' class='sub mut'>present</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A cube-like graph with one additional diagonal edge creating an odd cycle, vertices coloured with 3 colours (green, purple, orange) showing a valid 3-colouring.

given.aim: Find the chromatic number of the given graph which contains an odd cycle.

terms.meaning: The undirected graph shown in the GATE IT 2008 question, a modified cube-like structure with an additional edge.
terms.example: Outer square + inner square + connecting edges + one diagonal.
terms.connects: The added diagonal creates an odd cycle.

terms.meaning: A cycle with an odd number of edges/vertices in the graph.
terms.example: The diagonal edge connects two vertices forming a triangle or 3-cycle.

terms.meaning: Assignment of 3 colours to vertices with no two adjacent vertices sharing a colour.
terms.example: Assign colours 1, 2, 3 to resolve all conflict pairs.

given.plan: Show chi >= 3 by locating an odd cycle (ruling out 2-colourability), then exhibit an explicit 3-colouring to confirm chi = 3.

steps.title: Establish chi >= 3: find an odd cycle
steps.formula_raw: If G contains a cycle of odd length, then chi(G) >= 3.
steps.apply: The graph contains an odd cycle (the extra diagonal edge creates a triangle with two adjacent vertices). Therefore 2 colours are insufficient and chi >= 3.
steps.note: A graph is bipartite iff it has no odd cycle; since this graph has one, chi >= 3.

steps.title: Exhibit a valid 3-colouring: chi <= 3
steps.formula_raw: Assign colours greedily or by inspection to all vertices using at most 3 colours.
steps.apply: Label vertices and assign colour 1, 2, or 3 systematically: the outer ring and inner vertices can be coloured with 3 colours respecting all adjacencies. No vertex requires a 4th colour.
steps.note: The graph has maximum clique size 3 (a triangle), confirming chi = 3 = clique number.

questions.formula_note: The odd-cycle criterion establishes chi >= 3, while the explicit 3-colouring closes the argument at chi = 3; the clique lower bound (triangle => chi >= 3) gives the same result.

questions.question:
Specify an adjacency-lists representation of the undirected graph given above.

[Graph: An undirected graph with vertices {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and edges visible in the figure. The graph shows a small connected graph with edges such as 1-2, 1-3, 2-4, 3-4, 3-5, 4-5.]
questions.to_find: Adjacency-lists representation of the given undirected graph

understand.plain: An adjacency-list representation of a graph stores, for each vertex, the list of its neighbours. For an undirected graph, if vertex u is in v's list then v must also appear in u's list. We read off the edges from the figure and build each vertex's neighbour list.

keywords.explain: A data structure representing a graph as an array of lists, where list[v] contains all neighbours of vertex v.
keywords.example: For edge (1,2): vertex 1's list includes 2, and vertex 2's list includes 1.

keywords.explain: A graph where each edge (u,v) is bidirectional; both u and v appear in each other's adjacency lists.
keywords.example: Edge 3-5 means 5 is in list[3] and 3 is in list[5].

keywords.explain: Adjacency lists require O(V + E) space, which is efficient for sparse graphs.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 360 280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='280' rx='12' class='bg'/><line x1='80' y1='80' x2='160' y2='40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='80' x2='80' y2='180' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='240' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='180' x2='240' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='180' x2='160' y2='220' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='140' x2='160' y2='220' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='80' cy='80' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='160' cy='40' r='14' class='hi'/><circle cx='80' cy='180' r='14' class='hi'/><circle cx='240' cy='140' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='160' cy='220' r='14' class='ok'/><text x='80' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>1</text><text x='160' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>2</text><text x='80' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>3</text><text x='240' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>4</text><text x='160' y='225' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>5</text><text x='280' y='40' class='sub mut'>1: 2, 3</text><text x='280' y='60' class='sub mut'>2: 1, 4</text><text x='280' y='80' class='sub mut'>3: 1, 4, 5</text><text x='280' y='100' class='sub mut'>4: 2, 3, 5</text><text x='280' y='120' class='sub mut'>5: 3, 4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 5-vertex undirected graph with edges 1-2, 1-3, 2-4, 3-4, 3-5, 4-5, with the adjacency list representation shown on the right side.

given.aim: Construct the adjacency-list representation for each vertex of the given undirected graph.

terms.meaning: The nodes of the graph, here numbered 1 through 5.
terms.example: Vertex 3 is connected to vertices 1, 4, and 5.
terms.connects: Each vertex gets its own adjacency list.

terms.meaning: The connections between vertex pairs, read from the figure: {1-2, 1-3, 2-4, 3-4, 3-5, 4-5}.
terms.example: Edge 2-4 means 4 appears in list[2] and 2 appears in list[4].
terms.connects: Edges are the entries added to adjacency lists.

terms.meaning: In an undirected graph, edge (u,v) adds v to list[u] AND u to list[v].
terms.example: Edge 3-5 adds 5 to list[3] and 3 to list[5].
terms.connects: Ensures symmetric representation for undirected graphs.

given.plan: List each vertex from 1 to 5 and enumerate its neighbours from the graph figure. Since the graph is undirected, each edge (u,v) contributes one entry to each of list[u] and list[v].

steps.title: Read edges from the graph
steps.apply: From the figure: vertex 1 connects to 2 and 3; vertex 2 connects to 1 and 4; vertex 3 connects to 1, 4, and 5; vertex 4 connects to 2, 3, and 5; vertex 5 connects to 3 and 4.
steps.note: There are 6 edges in total; each appears twice in the combined adjacency lists.

steps.title: Write the adjacency lists
steps.formula_raw: list[v] = sorted list of neighbours of v
steps.note: Total entries = 2 * |E| = 12 (each edge counted twice for undirected graph).

questions.formula_note: No special formula is needed; the construction directly follows the definition of adjacency lists: for each edge (u,v) in an undirected graph, add v to list[u] and u to list[v].
